speaker: interviewer
text: Number six. It was the spring of '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were working with-- for the Pawluks?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Pawluks. And occasionally, they allowed me-- they gave me some products which I could smuggle in. All the food that I brought was given to Rosa, because she was recuperating from typhoid. And one morning, later part of the morning, Mr. Pawluk came from his place of work. He says-- my Christian name was Paulina-- Paulina, don't get out into the courtyard. Don't stick your head out, because there is a pogrom-- a-- an action in ghetto, a liquidation of the ghetto. Don't go out of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- Mrs.-- Mrs. Pawluk was very nervous because they were-- they were informing the people that they-- that they are not allowed to give shelter to Jews. If they catch a Christian family hiding a Jew, they'll be shot. He-- so she said, what's going to happen? We're going to have her here. He said, she has Christian documents. We must pretend that we knew that she was Christian. And so when Mr. Pawluk told me that there is a action taking place in the ghetto, my heart sank. What's going to happen to my family now? They must be hiding someplace in a cellar or maybe on the rooftop, and they're going to get them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went on with my chores just like-- without-- not being able to function. He came home that evening. We heard-- we even heard shots because it was a small town. They were rounding them up, taking them off to the ghetto, lining them-- them up in rows, and leading them to transports where were freight trains ready to-- to extermination camps. Because from our town, they didn't have like Auschwitz where some people survived and worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: From our town, it was a death camp where directly from the transport they unloaded them and directly led them to the gas chambers. I was numb, thinking of my parents, what's going to happen. But at the same time, what's going to happen to me? They're not going to let me stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mrs. Pawluk was very nervous keeping me there. And they were discussing it, because now there were all over town, signs, not that-- on the death penalty to give any shelter to a Jew. So she was-- for the sake of her children-- she was nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So suddenly, I-- when I heard them talking in the next room, and she was nervous, we can't have her here, I got my little valise that I had and started packing up. And get-- getting to the door, thanking them for their kindness. I'm leaving. And she said, where are you going? I said, you have been very kind to me, but I realize I cannot endanger you and your family. So I'm going. She said, but where are you going? So I said goodbye. I reached for her hand to kiss. She was too embarrassed because she was good to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- as I was going out, she said, wait a minute. You go out and they'll kill you. They'll shoot you. I can't let you go. I have to think. She drew me back into the house, and I stood there with my little valise. She said, I have to think. And then an idea came. Next to her house was-- her mother lived and her sister, who was going to-- attending university in Lemberg, Ukrainian. There, life was normal. And it was around the Easter holidays, she came for a vacation. And she's leaving tonight with a friend. Maybe-- they were thinking, they have a relative near Tarnopol who is a teacher in a small village. Maybe Lydia could use a girl to take care of her children. Lydia is a good person, and maybe she find a place. We'll send her off there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's going to buy her ticket? Maria, her sister, who is going back to Lvov, Lemberg. So it was decided, and that was a good idea. So she came and she said, you're going to go leave town with my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she brought me into the bedroom and shut the drapery. Nobody see. Stay there. Don't move. Don't go any place. Don't move. Don't go near a window. I was there for the day, and I heard shooting from a distance. I heard distant shooting, shooting. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Thinking of my parents, thinking-- at the same time, I have no time to think, because I am in danger. My instinct of survival is so strong. I was sitting there in the dark, the drapes drawn. And then when nightfall came-- she wanted me to eat, but I could not swallow. When nightfall came, her sister was ready with her girlfriend, with her luggage, and I with my valise. And we were walking towards the train station. The  trains are not well lit. Very darkened because they're afraid of enemy planes. So he-- the conductor just has a lantern. And they were very cautious not to have bright lights. She went in. I stayed inside. She went in and bought the ticket for me. She came out, gave me the ticket, wished me luck, and they disappeared. Now I'm passing the door, handing over the ticket. He just looked at the ticket, let me pass, and I'm sitting on a train, sitting by the window. It's dark. And I wished that that train should move already. I hear shooting from the ghetto. And as I was walking-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] as I was walking with the girls-- they had luggage. She had an overnight bag with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was walking, it was [GERMAN], the-- the-- the street that leads you to the train station. To the right, I saw the sky flaming red even though it was nighttime. When they were emptying out the ghetto, let-- leading out the people, there were still some people in the cellars. So to smoke them out, they set fires. The sky was flaming red.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked in that direction, and I was wondering if my-- my parents are stuck in a cellar, in flames. Knowing I'll never come back to this town. And what's going to happen to me? Am I going to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We reached the station. She told me to stand aside. She's going to go in and get the ticket. She gave me the ticket, wished me luck, and they disappeared. I went on a train and sat down. I passed by with my ticket and sat down. People were coming. There were Christian people who were going from ghetto to ghetto places, delivering foods, because that was their time to make money. For a bread, somebody took off a ring and gave them. They-- they became merchants. And the train was darkened because of the enemy planes, not to be seen. And so they had discussions. There were a few people talking about events that take place in the ghetto. Understand. And they were talking about Jews. And they said what they had seen. This one was killed, and this one was killed. And somebody said, Dr. Borgner, which I knew. He was a good man. When my wife was delivering the baby, I didn't have money. I was unemployed. And this doctor came to my house. And when I told him right in advance that I don't have money, he said, it's OK. You pay me when you have. And he delivered a baby. He even came several times for-- to check up.

speaker: interviewer
text: A man like that, I would have give him shelter and save his life. But I have three children now in a small place. How can you make children keep a secret? Where am I going to put him? My neighbors come in. He was a good man. They were telling who was shot and that, and who was this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then a fellow sitting near me, he lit a cigarette, and he saw a girl sitting. He started a conversation. He's asking me where am I going. I said to Tarnopol. Because near Tarnopol, Lydia was-- the relative of Mrs. Pawluk. He said, I am going there. So I said, I'm visiting relatives. We were talking. And all he was talking about, the events that take place about the Jews and so. And then one of the men said, I know that this stations-- on every station, Germans are posted.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, why? Because there might be some Jews trying to smuggle through. So that was in my mind. It was in my mind that if I get off, maybe they'll catch me. I have my Christian documents on me, but on the-- do I know if they'll perhaps accuse me? So I was very nervous.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this fellow was talking to me, flirting with me. And as we're getting-- the train dragged the whole night with stops. Normally, it would not have taken this long. It was daylight, already, morning. And he got off with me, and he took my valise, holding. I felt safer. Like I'm passing through, looking into his face and smiling. There was-- there were Germans stationed. They did not search everyone. But if somebody would have looked suspicious, they would have stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I was laughing in-- into his face, and I passed that turn. What do you call? that-- that-- where you go through. And as we came out, he said, can he see me the next day or something? I said, no, I'm visiting my relatives just for a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Said goodbye, and I went. Now I'm walking through Tarnopol, and the same process is taking place there. It was like an order from Berlin, liquidating the Jews. There's-- you see-- you hear shooting. You see people being led under guard. The difference is that I am not from this town. There are no Christians who know me who will point me out. But I feel exposed. And I'm asking, which is the-- why way is to that village where I'm going, to be outside of the town as quickly as possible, to disappear. And I find myself at the outskirt of the town where there are fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I kept asking directions, where is that school? And they told me. I came there, and it was during school time. So Lydia was not there. So a neighbor opened the door. She said, you can wait here till she comes. So I went to her house. She did not ask questions. I said, I'm bringing regards from my home town to her, and so on. 2 o'clock, school was over. And she opened the door. She heard Lydia. Lydia, you have a guest. So I walked into Lydia's house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did not reveal to her that I'm Jewish. I just said that I'm looking for work, and her relative from my hometown told me that she may need somebody. And she said she has an older woman who is good with the children. The children love her. But she's going to look for me, and I'm welcome to stay in her house. So while-- I stayed there for three days, and she was looking. And then the principal-- it was-- they lived on the premises near the school, because this was a village. The principal and his wife had a baby. She had a girl taking care of the baby, but the girl was getting married. And just luckily for me, she needed somebody. They did not ask questions, and I was able to work for her. She was a good woman. While she was teaching-- she was a teacher, her husband was a principal-- I was taking care of the baby, preparing for supper, peeling potatoes and doing things. The village boys there from the neighborhood noticed me, because when it-- weather was-- later, when it was sunny, I took out the baby in the sun. They saw a young girl, and they wanted to meet me. And I was frightened, but there was nothing else to do. I walked out. I lied. I'm Roman Catholic, and so on. That they are rounding up young girls to Germany to labor, because the-- because they mobilized all the men. They are short of manpower, so they are catching young girls to go work in their factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said that my stepmother would like to get rid of me, and I don't want to go to Germany because Germany's being bombed day and night. So that I-- that's why I'm working. He said, oh, you're so smart. They liked me. They were playing the harmonica and talking and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for a few days, I felt-- for a few weeks, I felt safe. It was a sort of remote village. I'm trying not to get into-- much into conversation. I don't even want them to know that I'm working because I don't look like a peasant working. They may think I'm related. I'm just coming here for the summer. And suddenly, something terrible happened. Her husband, Vincent, had a heart attack. They rushed him to Tarnopol to the hospital. After a few days, he died. Now Sophia, she went to stay with him. So I was left in my house-- in the house taking care of the baby. She was very appreciative that she has somebody to leave her baby with. I was very good with the baby. After he died, and they brought him back to that village where the funeral took place. And her mother came, her family came, and there was all that procedure where people come, and so on. She was heartbroken, completely lost. And she appreciated my being-- taking care of baby. But then they decided-- and the mother said, you are alone here with the baby. Come live with us. That she should move to go with the family now that she's a widow. And it was decided that Sophia will go to live with her mother. Her life has changed. She has no need to be in a village. She could get a job, probably, there. So my job is at stake, and she felt very badly. And Lydia came. Something else-- Lydia came. Lydia-- I brought-- when I came to Lydia, I brought regards from the Pawluks. And at one time-- it was like vacation time and holiday-- she went to Brody. She said she's going to Brody to visit the family. I told her that my-- my-- my mother's dead, my father remarried, and my stepmother would like me to go to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's-- she would like to get rid of me. That's why I'm working. And Lydia went to Brody. She said, do you want me to say-- give regards to your father and your stepmother? I said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: When she visited the Pawluks, they told her the truth. When she came back-- that was before Vincent died. When she came back, she brought me regards from my brothers. And I thought, my god, she knows about me. The Pawluks told her the truth. My brothers were at that time in hiding, because their organization where they-- where they were stationed, where they were working-- there was-- my Zigi, my brother, was receiving food from the kitchen. And the chef, the German chef, liked my brother, my twin brother. He said, you resemble my Hans, my son. He showed him a picture. My brother looked like him, and he had sentiment for him. He gave him extra portions. One day, he told him that tomorrow morning, the-- that camp is going to be liquidated. That-- it's the end of that from the [GERMAN], they had orders that they have to remove those-- that-- those people. My brother-- and don't tell anybody, he told him. My brother told everybody. They stayed in a one-story building where the one window was to the back. And it was on a second floor. No fire escapes. So they were not guarding that place. So they took sheets and tied it into ropes, and let themselves down. My two brothers went down at first, and then the others. That in the morning, they're going to liquidate that-- that camp. They-- they are moving on, and they don't need any more that road work for them. My brothers started to run as they let themselves down, and the others are to follow. But as they were running, they heard shots, which means they were discovered. My brother-- my brothers ran and wound up in a bombed out building and a cellar under the rubble, and they stayed there. Later-- but they stayed there for several days, and later they were hungry. They had to go look for places. And we knew that we have a-- a fellow, Janic Kowalski, that we knew. And they figure at night, they're going to go. Maybe he'll give them some bread or something. He's a good man. He's not going to point them out. And they begged he should give them a few days-- shelter for a few days. And then they're going to look for another place. It was endangering him, but there were some kind people. And my brothers stayed there a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were-- they knew that I am at Pawluks. And they said, is she alive? They knew that they-- they are liquidating the ghetto. So he was kind enough to go to the Pawluks and find out. And the Pawluks told him that they sent me away, and that I am near Tarnopol in a place. My brothers were very happy that I'm alive and I was-- that they didn't throw me out, just into the street. And at the time when this Kowalski came to find out, Lydia was visiting them. And she was going back to Tarnopol. So when he came back and told them all that, my brother, Muno, my older brother, remembered that my shoes were torn. I had no shoes. If I have to run, what am I going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He begged that Kowalski, that Janic, he should get a pair-- he knew my size, more or less. My twin brother knew. To get a pair of shoes. Even if they are bigger, I'll wear them. For me. And he got a pair high button shoes, like new shoes, with laces. And he brought it to the Pawluks. And Lydia came. And when she came back, she looked for opportunity where Mrs.-- where Sophia was not in the house. She came to see me, and she said, I know everything about you. And I know about your brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I heard of my brothers, I was crying. Are they alive? Did you see them? She said she did not see them, but she knows that they are alive. And a man came, and they are in hiding. And I have something for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she opened up a box with high laced, black, beautiful shoes, which I needed to go to church. My shoes were like-- the sole was coming off. I tied it with a string. She brought me the shoes and regards. I cried and so on. And she said she knows everything about me. And I shouldn't worry. She's not going to throw me out into the street. Now I'm going back to the situation where the-- Sophia's husband died. And they're packing, and they're leaving. So for few days, I stayed with Lydia. And she decided-- her husband was not with them because he had a-- a store, a small store, in Tarnopol. And we can see what come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took a horse and wagon with a farmer, and she drove me to Tarnopol and left me at her husband's house. And he was very kind. I stayed overnight there, and he's going to get me a ticket. She was thinking, what should we do? Where should she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She cannot hold me. There's nowhere in the village, room for me, a job for me. In Buchach. She has an aunt in Buchach. Her husband is a veterinarian. She's very kind. She's the kindest woman. She'll send me to her. She's going to help me. It's like sending on a relative a problem like me, but she was kind, helping me. So the second time, I was sent off to a relative. He bought me a ticket, brought me to the train, and disappeared. I took a train to Buchach. It was a scenically nice little town. There was a river strip, a contribute-- a tributary to the river-- the Vistula, which goes to the Baltic Sea. Very scenically. Hilly on both sides. The little bridge, two-- separated both sides of the town, and there a hilly area. With-- so-- I came to the center of the town, and there was a very impressive building. Downstairs, there was a pharmacy. And I walked up, came to Mrs.-- Mrs. Popel was her name. I knocked on the door, and a peasant girl opened the door. I said, I am here to see Mrs. Popel, Maria Popel. She said, somebody is here to see you. So Mrs. Popel, a attractive woman in her 50s, came out. And she invited me in. I said, I bring regards from Lydia. And so on. I cannot talk to her. The maid is listening. She recognized. She said, Hania, make coffee for us. And I told her. And she-- my-- Mrs. Lydia told me, tell her the truth. Don't lie to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told her. And she said, you poor thing, so young. You have to fight for your life. I'm going to see what I can do. And she made me something to eat. She took me in. She said, don't tell-- Hania shouldn't hear it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she decided she's going to look for something for work for me. The next day-- her husband came home. Quiet man. A veterinarian, I said. The next day, he was going to farms where he inspects the cattle and things. He travels. He comes and goes. We went out to look, through her friends to look if somebody needs some work. There were promises, but nothing particular. Two days passed. On a third day, I-- we are coming home from some-- there was a prospect that there's a woman that needs, but she was not at home. Mrs. Wiener needs somebody. So we'll go tomorrow. As we were walking up the steps, we hear German voices. Immediately, it's danger, and I run back. I run out into the street. Mrs. Popel went upstairs to her apartment, and she saw the Germans. And she-- they were looking for a Jewish girl. They-- they were told that there's a Jewish girl working here, by her-- in her employment. Hania did not speak German, to my luck. She could not say anything. So Mrs. Popel said, my husband is a veterinarian. He's prominent. We would not do anything that is illegal and endanger ourself. This is my maid. I don't employ anybody. I have no children. And they excused themselves and left. I was-- ran out a block down. There was a park and a bench a block down, and I was sitting, shivering, afraid what's happening there. Did not come out. Stayed there for a long time. And it was already late afternoon, Mrs. Popel appeared. She said, stay there till dark. Don't come up. They were looking for you. Somebody reported. I'm going to find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then she started to interrogate Hania. Hania, Paulina came right from the station. Nobody knew. Nobody knows her. Somebody reported her. I said, but who knew? How would Hania know? We were whispering in the next room. We-- the door was closed. How would she know that I'm Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: She started to get the truth out of her. Hania, get your things and get this instant out of my house, if you don't tell me the truth. Hania started to cry. She had a boyfriend, a loudmouth, and she told him that she thinks there's another girl that is going to take her job. She's worried about her job. And she may be Jewish. She doesn't look the part. She doesn't look like a maid. I was delicate. He went and reported to the German police.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she cried and wept. And Mrs. Popel said to her, do you know what you did? Do you realize what you did? You endangered a priest. They're going to shoot him on account of you. And Hania, bewildered, said why? What-- what is a priest? She said, who do you think gave Paulina the documents? A Polish priest. If they catch her and they interrogate her, she is going to tell them-- have to tell them where she obtained the documents. The priest is going to be shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Peasants, particularly, are very religious. And she started to cry. Oh, my god, a priest. I don't want that. She said, in that case, you must forget Paulina. You must forget what you know about her for the sake of the priest. Because if a priest is going to be shot, you're going to go to hell after death. Peasants usually believe all that very strongly. And she wept and cried. She said, I told my boyfriend I thought maybe she's going to work for you and you wouldn't-- she said, she's not going to take your place. Don't tell anybody, because every time you have a thought of telling, you're going to kill a priest. So-- and then she told her boyfriend, I made a mistake. She's not Jewish. And she left. So that was it. And then when it turned dark, Mrs. Popel came to me-- fetched me-- not to go into the house. She still didn't trust. Maybe the Germans would come back, maybe watch. She-- we sneak by the door, and she brought me up on the rooftop, being it was warm weather. I waited. Then she came up with a pad and a blanket. Later at night, she came and brought me a glass of milk and a sandwich. And I spent the night on the roof. And we were supposed to go to Mrs. Wiener the next day. And then she was afraid when she goes through with me, their neighbors, they may see, suspect. So she went out and I followed her. She went a block, and then we met. We went to Mrs. Wiener's house. It was a little bit away from the central of the city, from the center of the city. And I walked into Mrs. Wiener's house. Mrs. Wiener was a widow whose husband was-- had a high position. And under normal circumstances, she would have had a pension. But by the Germans, she did not. She had one daughter, Millie, who worked for a German-- German-- German commandant there. She had a position there. And she lost her son. The-- her daughter was her life. In order to survive, in order to be able to exist, she had boarders in her house, girls from the adjoining villages who went to school. And the boarders-- the boarders, she cooked for them, and they paid her. In order to be able to feed those girls, she had a cow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a private house with a stall, a cow. She had chickens. And from the boarder girls-- from the cow, she had milk. She could sell some milk. She lived. And her daughter had a job by the Germans, so they managed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- as I-- she was talking, Mrs. Popel left me with her. There was a little girl like 12 years old, very cute little girl. She came in. And she said to Mrs. Wiener, I-- I already cleaned up the stall, and I fetched water for the cow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, what can I do now? She said, bring-- bring up potatoes from the cellar to peel. So she went to the cellar to get-- fetch potatoes. Mrs. Wiener walked into the next room. A neighbor, next door nosey neighbor walked in and she saw me. I don't know what Mrs. Wiener take-- took her time there. She saw me. She said to me, you know that little girl there? She's Jewish.
